name: Adjective Agreement
summary: Italian adjectives change their ending to match the gender and number of the noun.
explanation:
Italian adjectives agree with the noun in gender and number. Most adjectives end in -o and have four forms: -o (masc. singular), -a (fem. singular), -i (masc. plural), -e (fem. plural). So alto (tall) becomes alto, alta, alti, alte.

A second group ends in -e and has only two forms: -e (singular, both genders) and -i (plural). So grande (big) becomes grande and grandi.

Adjectives usually come after the noun (una macchina rossa, a red car), though a few common ones can come before.
commonMistakes: Leaving the adjective in its dictionary -o form regardless of the noun.
Putting the colour or description before the noun. Italian usually says casa bianca, not bianca casa.
Forgetting that -e adjectives still change in the plural (grande to grandi).
Making a feminine plural end in -i. It should be -e (le ragazze italiane).
Matching the adjective to the English noun instead of the Italian one.
tip: Mismatched genders take the masculine plural: Marco e Maria sono italiani.
